title: Cookies, Local Storage, Session Storage
question:
What is the difference between:
Cookies
Local Storage
Session Storage
answer:
Cookies
Limit 4kb
Server and client side
*Nice for http authentication 
*Good for tokens or anything you want to share 

Local storage 
Higher limit
Persistent for ever
Application state

Session
Does deleted when we close the tab 
When you want to store form inputs

title: Setting up frontend App
question: If you are setting up a frontend application, what are some optimizations you would put in place in order to make it more performant?
answer:
1 polyfill 
This is the browserfication of the project, we have different versions for different browsers making all able to read the project as the same way
- tree shaking 
This happens by default in the module bundlers to make sure we are using the right modules, so we need ES6 modules for doing tree shaking in our code

2 compression
We should bundle files into a gzip or brotli, so we can send it a smaller file that a browser can decompress and read it, this should lower down 70% of the total payload 

- Uglify + minification 
This removes all words and transform into a code without any need for a human to read, this speeds up as machine will read a shorter code from the original written


- Source maps
They are extra files that will be pushing the original part of each code, so we can debug at the browser level (production).

3 Code splitting 
We only ship the js necessary for the initial load and we can lazy load or defer that not strictly needed for the initial load 

-------------------------------

Outside of the code

- add it to a CD
- optimizing images size 
- optimizing fonts

title: Image optimizations
question: You are building with very big images, maybe is a e-commerce store with high definition pictures, how would you optimize this for maximum performance?
answer:
Dimensions: minimum
- should exist an image with smaller size and still keep the information at image after compression of it (you don't want to show pixels at your image)

Compression & Image Optimizations 
- tools can remove the metadata or you can modify the color space to include less colors, get less vibrant but it's a smaller image

Compressions:
1 - brotli
2 - gzip
3 - deflate

WebP
Whenever we can we should use this format for all images, it's the most recommend 

CDN - caching policy
You can add your images or any kind of static file so a person living in another part of the world can access from this CD instead of the current server


Lazy loading 
Images load when needed

Load on scroll
It's an example of lazing load, as we load when a person scroll, so each new image only will be downloaded when a certain part of the page is reached 

Use width and height 
You can specify specific sizes for each display (pc,mobile, tablet)

SrcSet
You can define different files for specific devices(viewport), so when you are on a mobile view you would get an even smaller size due to the mobile resolution

title: Code quality
question:
How would you manage code quality in a large scale frontend application? 

What tools would you use?
answer:
I would manage code quality using a mix of standards, automation, testing, security checks, and monitoring.

1. Code standards

I would use:

- ESLint
- Prettier
- TypeScript strict mode
- Angular style guide rules

These tools help keep the code consistent across the team and catch small problems early, before code review.

I would also include accessibility rules in the lint setup, so the team can catch common a11y issues during development.

2. Testing

I would use different levels of testing:

- Unit tests for components, services, pipes, and utilities
- Integration tests for connected frontend flows
- E2E tests for critical user journeys

For Angular, this could include:

- Jest or Karma for unit tests
- Cypress or Playwright for E2E tests

The goal is not to test everything blindly, but to protect the most important business flows.

3. Dependency security

I would use dependency scanning tools like:

- npm audit
- Snyk
- Dependabot

This helps detect vulnerable or outdated packages before they become a security risk.

4. CI/CD checks

I would make quality checks part of the pipeline:

- lint
- format check
- unit tests
- build validation
- dependency scan
- E2E tests for important flows

This avoids relying only on developers remembering to run checks locally.

5. Performance and monitoring

I would use tools like:

- Lighthouse Metrics
- Google Pagespeed

These tools help track frontend performance, runtime errors, and user experience problems after deployment.

Interview answer

In a large-scale Angular application, I would manage code quality by combining automated checks and team standards. I would use ESLint, Prettier, TypeScript strict mode, and accessibility lint rules to keep the code consistent. I would add unit tests and E2E tests to protect important flows, use dependency scanning tools like Snyk or Dependabot for package security, and enforce everything through the CI pipeline. After deployment, I would also use tools like Lighthouse Metrics or Google Pagespeed to monitor performance.

title: XSS
question:
What is an XSS Attack? 

How can you be sure that your frontend application is not vulnerable to this attack?
answer:
Cross Side Scripting 
The attacker persist some code into the project database 

The users fetch things into the database can fetch this code

Example:
Facebook
- people could add JavaScript code as a comment
- while people opening the thread, they would need to load all comments, making it run the malicious script 


How to prevent?

1- sanitize the inputs ( we avoid the process of allowing to add this as JavaScript)
2- we remove the option for users render any HTML/js, so even if is stored, it wouldn't run the malicious code

title: CDN how they work?
question:
How does CDN work? 

What are some disadvantages, advantages of having a CDN? 

What are common providers?
answer:
CDN - content delivery network 
This is basically a network of servers around the world that replicate the website, so if someone is too far away from the current server they would be using a cached version into the edge location at CDN, and those would only update when we lunch new versions of the system. 

Common Providers 
- Claudfare 
- AWS Cloud Front 
- Azure CDN

title: Micro frontends
question:
What are micro-frontends? 

When would you use this kind of frontend architecture?
answer:
Micro frontends 
They are the solution as a split of one project into multiple ones, those micro-frontends are managed by the application (increase complexity), and they allow multiple people to work in a bigger project. 

When would we use? 
When a company grows so much the quantity of front end developers that are the need to split the code so people can work separately without the worry of breaking the main code.

title: 5 libraries
question: If you had to pick 5 libraries as dependencies for your Angular App which ones would you pick?
answer:
Prettier (eslint or tslint)
Allows us to format our code, so everyone in the team can have the same code format, and get some issues done by the tslint

Angular material 

Jest

Sanitise (DOMSanitize)

RXJS (observables)

@elastic/elasticsearch

title: Debugging
question: What is your go-to strategy for debugging Angular Components?
answer:
UI erros 
I can log with string interpolation (or console log) some signals or state so I can see what was rendered, so I can have an idea of where is the bug itself

I would likely add some debugger; statements at the points where the issue occurs so I can pause execution and inspect the data flowing through each involved element

From there, I would inspect the component state, inputs, outputs, and data flow using source maps and browser developer tools to identify where the incorrect data is introduced, then fix the flow to ensure the application behaves correctly.

If is something injector related:
I would use angular dev tools to see the injector hierarchy

title: Rxjs - Operators
question: Have you ever created a custom RxJS operator? What problem did you solve?
answer:
It was an operator that basically filtered out non-alphanumeric keystrokes.

For example, when working with an input, you may only want to react to numeric values or specific valid keys.

The reason for this was a dropdown component with custom search support. 

While the user types, we only want to trigger the search for relevant keys, and avoid running the search logic for keys like Shift, Ctrl, Arrow keys, or other keydown events that are not actual input characters.

title: Angular challenge
question: What unconventional challenges did you have while developing/architecting Angular Apps?
answer:
One unconventional challenge:

I had at Blocworx was transforming code that was heavily written inside HTML into a proper Angular application. 

I had to analyze the existing structure, understand the behavior hidden in the markup, and move that logic into Angular components, services, bindings, and reusable patterns.

The challenge was not just migration, but improving the architecture so the application became easier to maintain, test, and extend.

title: Optimization of large Angular Components
question: What is your approach to optimizing the performance of large angular components?
answer:
My approach is first understanding why the component is slow by inspecting templates, bindings, subscriptions, loops, and change detection. 

Then I reduce complexity by splitting large components into smaller ones and optimizing rendering with techniques like OnPush, trackBy, proper observable cleanup, and moving heavy logic outside the template.

For me, optimization is also about improving maintainability and readability, not only speed.

I also would check if something super big is stucking, if does I would defer and lazy load.

title: Large Angular application
question: How do you usually structure large Angular applications to keep them scalable and maintainable over time?
answer:
I usually organize Angular projects by features, not only by technical folders.

For example:
- auth
- dashboard
- users
- questions

Each feature contains its own:
- components
- services
- routes
- models

I keep reusable things separated into a shared area:
- shared components
- pipes
- directives
- utility functions

Core application logic stays in a core area:
- authentication
- guards
- interceptors

API configuration
I also try to keep responsibilities small:
- avoid massive components
- avoid services doing too many things
- split logic when needed

My main goal is:
- easier maintenance
- easier onboarding for developers
- easier testing
- clearer project structure
- better long-term scalability

title: Angular Lifecycle
question: What is the Angular component lifecycle, and why is it important?
answer:
- The Angular lifecycle is the sequence of stages a component goes through from creation until destruction.

- Angular provides lifecycle hooks that allow us to execute logic at specific moments of the component life.

- Some common hooks are:
  - ngOnInit
  - ngOnChanges
  - ngAfterViewInit
  - ngOnDestroy

- Example usages:

  - ngOnInit
    - load API data
    - initialize variables

  - ngAfterViewInit
    - access DOM elements
    - work with ViewChild

  - ngOnDestroy
    - unsubscribe observables
    - clear timers
    - prevent memory leaks

- For me, lifecycle hooks are important because they help organize the component behavior correctly during rendering and updates.

- They also help:
  - improve maintainability
  - avoid performance problems
  - avoid memory leaks
  - control rendering flow properly

title: Pros and cons
question: Tell me the pros and cons of Angular?
answer:
Pros

- fully supported 
- long term support
- typescript by default 

Cons
- level of entrance it's higher, so you need to learn a lot more
- codebase is bigger

title: AOT
question:
What is a AOT compilation? 

What are the benefits?
answer:
AOT - Ahead of Time compilation 

In Angular we have AOT and JIT(Just in time compilation)

JIT we use in development mode, which means we are compiling everything inside run-time. 

AOT compilation we use for production environments,  so we are preparing everything so we transpiling all the stuff before push it to production 

Benefits 

- faster as everything is compiled and compressed for a live environment 
- less requests as they are bundled into huge chunks application 
- is more strict (as all things should be working with current deployment)

title: Dependency Injection
question: What is dependency Injection? And why do we need it?
answer:
We have angular modules working with dependency Injection, means:
- each module has isolated scope
- we use specific modules, and we need to configure it in case to use a new dependency into your new class contractor 
- we strictly say what is allowed from what is not

title: Lifecycle Hooks
question:
What are lifecycle Hooks? 

What are the most popular of them?
answer:
Are methods that are happening though different phrases of creating Angular Components, they are several of them that are popular:

- ngOninit: this triggers when we are creating a new component, runs after the constructor is finished. 

- ngChange: this will trigger when we change our inputs data

-ngDestroy: this will trigger when the component is destroyed, a good example is when someone click to open another URL the previous component will be destroyed to get the new one on screen, usually we unsubscribe in this action.

- ngAfterViewInit: this will render when all childrens are done inside of this component, so this will trigger after that

title: Observables & Promises
question: What are the difference between promises and observables?
answer:
Promises only are trigger once by default, observables can be triggering many times when the data is changed its value the observable updates the value. 

Inside of angular you didn't need to work with promises, just with streams.

title: Sharing between components
question: How can we share data between components?
answer:
We have 3 different ways:

- inputs and outputs (we can pass data between parent and child component)

- services: they are classes that exist outside of the component and all our components can be using this services 

- State management: this is a solution that can be subscribed and when it does the data can be passed by

title: Component & Directive
question: What is the difference between component and directive?
answer:
- A component is a directive with its own template.

- Components are used to create visible UI parts of the application:
  - pages
  - cards
  - modals
  - forms

- Directives are used to add behaviour or manipulate existing elements.

- Angular has two common directive types:
  - structural directives
    - *ngIf
    - *ngFor
  - attribute directives
    - ngClass
    - ngStyle

- In simple terms:
  - components create UI
  - directives enhance or modify behavior of existing UI elements

- I usually use directives when I want reusable behavior without creating an entirely new component.

title: Async
question: What is the async pipe inside angular?
answer:
- The async pipe is an Angular pipe used to automatically subscribe to asynchronous data like:
  - Observables
  - Promises

- It also automatically unsubscribes when the component is destroyed, helping prevent memory leaks.

- Instead of manually subscribing inside the component, we can bind the observable directly in the template.

- Example:
  - users$ | async

- I usually use the async pipe because it keeps templates cleaner and reduces manual subscription management inside the component.

title: IVY
question: What is angular IVY?
answer:
- Angular Ivy is the modern rendering and compilation engine introduced in Angular 9.

- It improved Angular performance by making:
  - builds faster
  - bundles smaller
  - debugging easier

- Compared to the old View Engine, Ivy generates more optimized code and improves development experience.

- In real projects, the main benefits I noticed were:
  - faster application builds
  - better performance
  - improved tree-shaking
  - easier maintenance and debugging

title: Component & Directive
question: Can a component Import a directive?

title: Security practices
question: What security practices and libraries do you use in Angular applications?
answer:
- For authentication and authorization, I usually work with:
  - JWT authentication
  - route guards
  - HTTP interceptors

- Libraries and tools I commonly use:
  - @auth0/angular-jwt
    - helps manage JWT tokens
    - automatically attaches tokens to requests
  - Angular Router Guards
    - protect routes based on authentication or roles
  - HttpInterceptor
    - centralize token handling
    - handle 401 and 403 errors
    - avoid duplicating security logic

- For form security and validation:
  - Angular Reactive Forms
    - validation
    - sanitization
    - controlled form state

- For XSS protection:
  - I rely mostly on Angular built-in sanitization
  - avoid dangerous things like:
    - direct DOM manipulation
    - unsafe innerHTML

- For API security:
  - HTTPS only
  - secure headers from backend
  - proper CORS configuration

- For dependency security:
  - npm audit
  - Snyk sometimes
  - keeping packages updated

- My approach is always:
  - frontend improves security experience
  - backend enforces the real security rules

title: XSS sanitization
question: What libraries do you use for XSS sanitization in Angular?
answer:
- In Angular, the most common protection already comes built into the framework through:
  - DomSanitizer

- Angular automatically sanitizes many things in templates, which already helps prevent XSS attacks.

- When additional sanitization is needed, the most common library I see being used is:
  - DOMPurify

- DOMPurify is widely used to sanitize:
  - HTML content
  - rich text editor output
  - user-generated content

- Example use cases:
  - comments
  - CMS content
  - markdown rendering
  - dynamic HTML from APIs

- My approach is:
  - trust Angular built-in sanitization first
  - use DOMPurify when handling dynamic or external HTML content
  - avoid bypassing Angular security unless absolutely necessary

title: ngOnChanges
question: When would you use ngOnChanges, and what kind of problems does it solve?

title: ngAfterViewInit
question: What is the purpose of ngAfterViewInit, and when is it useful?
answer:
- ngAfterViewInit runs after Angular finishes creating the component view.
- I use it when I need to work with something from the template after it exists.
- Common use cases:
  - @ViewChild
  - focusing an input
  - initializing charts
  - connecting third-party UI libraries
  - reading DOM element sizes

Interview answer

I use ngAfterViewInit when my logic depends on the component template being fully rendered. For example, if I need to access a ViewChild, focus an input, or initialize a third-party library that needs the DOM, this hook is the right place.

title: Angular Lifecycle Sequence
question: What are the Angular lifecycle hooks and their execution sequence?
answer:
Lifecycle order

1. constructor
  - Component class is created
2. ngOnChanges
  - Runs when @Input() values change
  - First hook called for inputs
3. ngOnInit
  - Component initialization logic
4. ngDoCheck
  - Custom change detection
5. ngAfterContentInit
  - Content projection initialized
6. ngAfterContentChecked
  - Projected content checked
7. ngAfterViewInit
  - Component view initialized
8. ngAfterViewChecked
  - Component view checked
9. ngOnDestroy
  - Cleanup before component removal

Commonly used hooks

- ngOnInit
  - API calls
  - initialize data
- ngOnChanges
  - react to input updates
- ngAfterViewInit
  - ViewChild
  - DOM access
  - charts/libs initialization
- ngOnDestroy
  - unsubscribe observables
  - clear intervals/timers

Less commonly used

- ngDoCheck
- ngAfterContentChecked
- ngAfterViewChecked

These are more advanced hooks usually related to custom change detection or deep rendering control.

Interview answer

Angular lifecycle hooks are the stages a component goes through from creation until destruction. The sequence usually starts with constructor, then ngOnChanges, ngOnInit, rendering hooks like ngAfterViewInit, and finally ngOnDestroy for cleanup. 

Sequence

constructor -> ngOnChanges -> ngOnInit -> ngAfterViewInit -> ngDestroy

I mostly use ngOnInit, ngOnChanges, ngAfterViewInit, and ngOnDestroy in real projects.
